task_completion.description: Load the populated places shapefile into the GIS software.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The layer 'ne_110m_populated_places' is visible in the Table of Contents/layer panel or the points are rendered on the map view.

task_completion.description: Open the Convex Hull geoprocessing tool.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A geoprocessing dialog or tool window specifically titled 'Convex Hull' (or similar equivalent in the GIS software) is open on the screen.

task_completion.description: Select the correct input layer for the Convex Hull tool.
task_completion.visual_evidence: Inside the Convex Hull tool dialog, the input layer dropdown or text field is set to 'ne_110m_populated_places'.

task_completion.description: Specify the correct output file path for the resulting shapefile.
task_completion.visual_evidence: Inside the Convex Hull tool dialog, the output file path is explicitly set to '/home/ga/gvsig_data/exports/convex_hull_cities.shp'.

task_completion.description: Execute the tool and generate the convex hull polygon.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A new layer (e.g., 'convex_hull_cities') appears in the layer panel, and/or a large polygon encompassing the global point dataset is visible on the map canvas.

integrity.description: The agent used the GIS graphical user interface to perform the task.
integrity.visual_evidence: The screenshots show interaction with a desktop GIS application (like gvSIG or QGIS), rather than a terminal window running command-line tools (e.g., ogr2ogr, python).

integrity.description: The resulting polygon is a genuine convex hull of the global data.
integrity.visual_evidence: The output polygon on the map should tightly wrap the outermost points of the global dataset (forming a complex polygon), rather than being a manually drawn simple rectangle or arbitrary shape.

integrity.description: The agent did not use a text editor to write a script to bypass GUI interaction.
integrity.visual_evidence: There are no text editors (like nano, vim, gedit) open with Python or bash scripts designed to compute the convex hull programmatically.

privileged_info_for_vlm: The input file is located at /home/ga/gvsig_data/cities/ne_110m_populated_places.shp. The output must be saved exactly to /home/ga/gvsig_data/exports/convex_hull_cities.shp. The output is expected to be a Polygon (ESRI shape type 5). The resulting convex hull should have a massive global extent, specifically a bounding box width of at least 300.0 degrees and a height of at least 100.0 degrees, visually covering most of the Earth's populated areas.
